---
title: "Southern Wax Myrtle (Morella cerifera)"
slug: "morella-cerifera"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/631130637/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Southern Wax Myrtle"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fagales"
tax_family: "Myricaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fagales", "Myricaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/631130637/medium.jpg" alt="Southern Wax Myrtle" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/coreytcallaghan">
coreytcallaghan</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/346249546">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fagales
-   **Family:** Myricaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=119956)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in a variety of habitats including wetlands, flatwoods, coastal areas, and disturbed sites.

## Natural history notes

An evergreen shrub or small tree characterized by its aromatic, lance-shaped leaves and clusters of small, waxy blue-gray berries. In Florida, it is commonly found in a variety of habitats including wetlands, flatwoods, coastal areas, and disturbed sites. It grows year-round in warm conditions and provides important food and cover for wildlife, while also being well adapted to a range of soil conditions.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/119956-Morella-cerifera)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/3942)
